items.headline: CIT Endorses CAPE Phase 1 for April 20 Launch
items.summary: Senior Judge Richard Eaton approved CBP's progress on the CAPE refund system, confirming the April 20, 2026 rollout date. CAPE components are now 60-85% complete and will process 63% of IEEPA entries. The system will handle unliquidated entries and those within the 90-day voluntary reliquidation period with a 45-day processing window.

items.headline: CBP Confirms 45-Day CAPE Processing Timeline
items.summary: CBP filed updated status showing CAPE will take up to 45 days to process refunds after entry liquidation. Nearly 27,000 importers (78% of affected entries worth $120 billion) have enrolled for electronic refunds. Refunds will only be delivered after entries liquidate, creating potential delays for importers.

items.headline: CAPE Phase 1 Expands to Include Suspended Entries
items.summary: CBP expanded Phase 1 eligibility to include entries marked 'Suspended,' 'Extended,' or 'Under Review,' increasing coverage from basic unliquidated entries. The claim portal is now 85% complete, but finally liquidated entries remain excluded pending Phase 2 development with no announced timeline.

items.headline: Section 232 Metals Framework Overhaul Takes Effect
items.summary: New unified Section 232 tariff structure effective today consolidates steel, aluminum and copper tariffs. Steel faces 50% ad valorem rates, while derivative articles require 15% steel content minimum. This affects CAPE processing for entries with multiple tariff types and may complicate refund calculations.

items.headline: Finally Liquidated Entries Now Covered by CIT Order
items.summary: Judge Eaton's March 27 amended order extends refund mandate to finally liquidated entries, ensuring no importers are time-barred from IEEPA refunds. CBP committed to addressing these in future CAPE phases but provided no development timeline for Phase 2 functionality.

items.headline: 24 States Challenge Section 122 Replacement Tariffs
items.summary: State attorneys general filed suit arguing Section 122 balance-of-payments requirements cannot be met in post-1976 floating exchange era. They claim Trump is conflating trade deficits with balance-of-payments crises. A ruling against Section 122 could create new refund pool when tariffs expire July 24, 2026.
